title: Marquesan Verbal Phrase Structure: TAM, Polarity, Derivation and Word Order
introduction:
In Marquesan, the verb phrase (VP) is built around a small closed class of preverbal particles expressing tense, aspect, mood (TAM) and polarity, followed by the verb root (optionally bearing derivational prefixes), then directional or aspectual particles, and finally its arguments.  Core pronouns remain as free words after the verb, and objects or obliques are introduced by the preposition i.  The canonical template is:

  [NEG] – [TAM] – V (– HAKA-) – [DIRECTIONAL] – [SUBJECT] – (i OBJECT …)

Below we outline each piece of this structure with representative examples.

sections.description:
Marquesan places a TAM particle immediately before the verb root.  Among the most common:
  • ua (or u, i) marks perfective past
  • e marks imperfective/non-past (habitual, future, or past progressive)
  • te … nei marks present progressive
  • a (or atahi a) marks inceptive or imperative

Examples:
  1. ua ’ite ’oe ia ’aua.  “Have you met them?”
     – ua “past perfective” + ’ite “meet” + ’oe “you” + ia ’aua “them.”

  2. e oka te ’enana i to ’atou kahu kanahau.  “People would wear beautiful clothes.”
     – e “imperfective/non-past” + oka “wear” + te ’enana “the people” + i … “beautiful clothes.”

  3. te maakau nei au i tuu kui.  “I am thinking of my mother.”
     – te … nei “present progressive” + maakau “think of” + au “I” + i tuu kui “of my mother.”

  4. e kai ’a te kō, enā me te ti’au’e.  “The ant was eating, and so was the fly.”
     – e “imperfective” + kai “eat” + ’a te kō “the ant,” emulated in coordination with enā me te ti’au’e “and the fly.”

  5. ’Ave ’oe ta ’oe, ’ave au i tā’u.  “You will carry yours; I will carry mine.”
     – ’Ave “carry” (with e implied) + pronouns + i-objects; e-particle marking non-past is omitted in juxtaposed future statements.

Negation uses the particle a’e (sometimes a’i), placed immediately before the TAM or directly before the verb if no TAM is present.  A focus particle a’e nei may follow the verb to mean “ever/never.”

Example:
  a’e ’oe e ’ite a’e nei ia ’ia.  “You’ve never met him.”
   – a’e “not” + e “imperfective” + ’ite “see/meet” + a’e nei “ever/never” + ia ’ia “him.”

In subordinate or coordinated clauses, a’e likewise precedes each verb it negates.

Marquesan derives causatives with the prefix haka- (sometimes ha’a-), meaning “cause to X.”  This prefix attaches directly to the verb root and precedes any directional particles.

Example:
  ’a, a haka’ite mai a’e.  “Oh, show it to me, please!”
   – ’a (imperative) + haka’ite “cause to see/show” + mai “towards speaker” + a’e “politeness particle.”

Other derivational prefixes (e.g. ha’a- for iterative or passive senses) may also appear, reflecting the verb’s valency changes.

Following the verb (and any derivational prefix), optional particles encode direction or aspect:
  • mai “towards the speaker”
  • atu “away from the speaker”
  • iho, mai, etc., for finer aspectual nuances

Example:
  ua tuku mai ’ia i titahi pukukai mea tamata.  “He gave me the fruits to try.”
   – ua “past perfective” + tuku “give” + mai “to me” + ’ia “me” + i … “to try.”

These particles can combine or stack to refine motion or aspect.

After the verb phrase, Marquesan places the subject (free pronoun or NP) and then objects or obliques, introduced by i.  Pronouns never cliticize to the verb.

Examples:
  1. ua ’ite ’oe ia ’aua.  “Have you met them?”
     – [TAM ua] [V ’ite] [SUBJ ’oe] [OBJ ia ’aua].

  2. e oka te ’enana i to ’atou kahu kanahau.  “People would wear beautiful clothes.”
     – e [V oka] [SUBJ te ’enana] [i-object to ’atou kahu kanahau].

  3. U popahi te ti’au’e i titahi kape ti’au’e e hano ti’ohi i hea te kō me ta ’ia ke’ā.  “The fly tasked one of his subordinates to go see where the ant and her rock were.”
     – U “past perfective” + popahi “task” + te ti’au’e “the fly” + i titahi kape ti’au’e “one subordinate” + e hano ti’ohi … “to go observe ….”

Prepositional markers (i, me, no, etc.) clearly signal argument roles and adjuncts.

Passive clauses use a special verb form (often with an added suffix) and the particle ’a to mark the grammatical subject, while the agent is introduced by e.

Example:
  Ua ha’ana’oa ’a te’ā ke’ā e te ’enana.  “That rock was buried by man.”
   – ua “past” + ha’ana’oa (passive “bury-ed”) + ’a + te’ā ke’ā “that rock” + e te ’enana “by the man.”

In passives, the patient becomes the ’a-marked NP and the agent follows e; tense and aspect remain on the verbal particle.
